daily_review.narrative:
## Split Day: Medium Bets Carry a Rough Low Tier

**3-3 overall, -4.5% ROI** — the medium confidence plays did the heavy lifting while low confidence dragged us down.

**MEDIUM TIER: 2-0, +90.9% ROI**

Both unders cashed comfortably. Minnesota-Detroit projected 209 and landed at 196, benefiting from Minnesota's slower pace (96.1) grinding down Detroit's usual tempo. Charlotte-Philadelphia was the sweatier win — projected 229, finished 232 — but squeaked under the 233 closing number. Philadelphia's defense has been leaky (allowing 116.5 points per 100 possessions), and Charlotte came in scoring efficiently (117.2), so this one played close to script.

**LOW TIER: 1-3, -52.3% ROI**

Rough stretch here. One loss fell within normal variance (under 8 points off projection). 

The two big misses tell different stories:

**IND @ MIA UNDER 221.5** — Projected 213, finished 226 (*** BIG MISS ***). The pregame setup looked solid with Miami's slower pace (98.9) and Indiana's solid defense (110.8). But the game ran hotter than expected, likely driven by Miami's offense clicking above their recent form (they came in at 113.5 efficiency but clearly exceeded that on the night). When both teams shoot well and the pace doesn't slow as much as projected, unders get torched.

**MIL @ BKN UNDER 230.5** — Projected 222, finished 234 (*** BIG MISS ***). Brooklyn's defense has been atrocious (allowing 118.9 points per 100 possessions), but the model expected Milwaukee's slower pace (99.4) to keep this manageable. Instead, the game played faster than projected and Milwaukee's offense — already elite at 119.8 — likely ran even hotter. When an elite offense faces a sieve defense and the tempo doesn't cooperate, the under has no chance.

**WHAT TO WATCH**

When projecting unders against weak defenses, pace control becomes everything. Both big misses featured defenses allowing 118+ points per 100 possessions, and in both cases the game tempo didn't slow enough to compensate. The medium tier wins both featured at least one team under 102 pace — that's the safety margin we need when targeting unders.

weekly_review.narrative:
# Week of March 23-28: Rough Waters

Not going to sugarcoat this one — we got worked. 19-25-1 overall with a -17.2% ROI is a losing week by any measure. The medium confidence bets absolutely cratered at 4-10-1 (-42.4%), which is concerning since those are supposed to be our bread and butter. Even the high confidence plays only went 2-1, so we didn't have enough volume there to dig out of the hole.

## WHAT WORKED

The DET @ NOP over (226.5) shows what the model does well when the ingredients align. New Orleans was playing at an elite pace (103.4) with both teams scoring efficiently — Pelicans at 115.2 offensive efficiency, Pistons at 114.6. Detroit on a back-to-back typically means tired legs and less defensive resistance. We projected 233.7, it landed at 237. Clean read on a pace-up, defense-optional game.

The high confidence plays, limited as they were, at least showed discipline. Going 2-1 there suggests when the model sees a truly strong edge, it's not completely off base.

## WHAT DIDN'T

The GSW @ DAL under (230.5) was a disaster — our biggest miss of the season by a mile. We projected 207.0, the game hit 268.0, a 61-point gap. Yes, it went to overtime (252 in regulation), but even the regulation total crushes our number by 45 points. 

Here's the problem: the feature data actually supported a lower total. Golden State's pace was slow (93.5), Dallas wasn't much faster (102.6), and both defenses were struggling but not catastrophically so (Dallas 118.2 defensive efficiency, Golden State 117.0). This wasn't a case of missing obvious pace-up signals. The model saw two teams that shouldn't combine for 230+ and got absolutely torched. Sometimes the game script just goes nuclear and there's no feature that would've caught it.

The medium confidence collapse is the real concern. 4-10-1 suggests we're finding edges that don't actually exist, or we're getting the direction right but the confidence calibration is off.

## MODEL HEALTH

Zero closing line value across the board is a red flag. 0% positive CLV with full coverage means we're not finding any market inefficiencies — we're essentially coin-flipping against sharp closing numbers. That's not sustainable.

The 61-point miss on Dallas/Golden State aside, I don't see a systematic directional bias yet. We're not consistently too high or too low. But the medium confidence bucket performing worse than low confidence bets suggests our probability calibration needs work.

## HEADING INTO NEXT WEEK

Watch how the model handles games with conflicting pace signals. The Dallas/Golden State miss came when we had one slow team (Golden State at 93.5) against a faster one (Dallas at 102.6). The model might be over-weighting the slower pace and missing when the faster team dictates tempo. 

Also keeping an eye on whether that CLV drought continues. If we're still at zero next week, we need to seriously question whether we're adding any value beyond what the market already knows.
